narrative.summary: You're on Bloor West in the Annex, walking into the younger sibling of Côte de Boeuf with the same Chef Teo Paul. There's a bar where you'll want to sit—pressed tight enough that you're sharing the space with whoever's next you, which at a French bistro feels right. The steak frites is the thing, and the same perfection that made the original hard to get is probably coming here too.

blocks.title: The Space
blocks.body: You're on Bloor West in the Annex, walking into the younger sibling of Côte de Boeuf with the same Chef Teo Paul calling shots. There's a bar where you'll want to sit—pressed tight enough that you can hear the couple next to you debating whether to order a second round of croquettes. The room hums with that just-opened energy, all walk-ins and three-hour waits that somehow collapse to 90 minutes if you're willing to perch.

blocks.title: The Food
blocks.body: This is French bistro cooking stripped to its best parts: steak frites, soppressata croquettes with a slick of béarnaise that cuts through the cream, the kind of food that makes you understand why people line up. Teo Paul knows how to work butter and acid, and the portions don't make you feel like you're paying for the idea of Paris. Pricing stays reasonable—you're not going to wince at the bill, which is rare for steak done this well.

blocks.title: Go When
blocks.body: Show up early—5:30 pm if you're smart—or be prepared to kill time in the neighbourhood and hope for a bar seat callback. This is the spot when you want French bistro cooking without the fuss of a reservation system that doesn't exist yet.

blocks.title: The Vibe
blocks.body:
⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ "So excited that I got to check out Brasserie Côte on their third day of soft opening. They’re the sister restaurant to Côte de Boeuf, with a similar concept of French bistro-type dishes. The whole experience had me wagging my tail.

It’s walk-in only - we arrived at 5:30 pm but were told it would be a ~3 hour wait. Ruff news, right? Fur-tunately, we got a call around 6:30 pm for seats at the bar, which was puptastic!

Food, drinks, and service were all on par with Côte de Boeuf (aka everything was pawsitively doglicious). Portions were decent and pricing was very reasonable.  We got:

Soppressata croquettes: crispy on the outside, with creamy soppressata filling, served with a béarnaise sauce (which, despite also being creamy, provided a nice tangy balance to the soppressata). These disappeared faster than a treat at a puppy party.

Mille-feuille potato with sardines: a potato pavé that was crispy on the outside with many layers of thin, tender slices of potato, topped with a big dollop of ajo crema and smoked sardines. This one definitely had us drooling for more!

Steak tartare: we got ours with fries, but it’s also available with duck-fat toasts. The tartare was well-seasoned, and the fries were crispy golden brown on the outside and fluffy on the inside. Served with a dollop of thick and creamy aioli for dipping - truly the ultimutt combo.

Halibut with clams: perfectly cooked fish, served with beurre blanc, velvety mashed potato, fried capers, and a breadcrumb topping. Could have used a touch more seasoning (my personal preference), but overall still very tasty.

Tarte tatin: large chunks of tender, juicy apples on a puff pastry crust, with a dollop of crème fraîche to balance the sweetness. A pawfect ending to a doglicious meal.

So happy this has opened here in the Annex and can’t wait to visit again.

5 woofs out of 5 and two paws up! 🐶🐾"

⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ "Excited to try this place, feels more spacious than the Ossington location. Fully packed by 5pm. Apps were delicious. They had off-menu specials, and we got the smoked mussels, which was served on a crispy bread. Fish on potato pave was new to me, an inspired combo for sure. The steak was super tender and juicy, and the sauce had a depth of flavour that is hard to describe. We were too full for dessert but still ordered the chocolate cake! West Annex/Koreatown is now bookended by this restaurant and Chantecler at Christie, the makings of a legit competitor to that stretch on Harbord west of Spadina."

⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ "First fine dining spot on Bloor since ... ever? Harbord and Dupont have Rasa, Parquette, Fat Pasha et al, but nobody's really thrown down on this patch of the subway line (W of Yorkville, E of Ossington) with an effort like this in some time. Sign of a good restaurant? When you bump into the chef smoking a cigarette outside the place at 10 in the morning and they don't open till dinner. The amount of work and effort that must have went into changing a 30 year landmark like BTW Café into this gem? I can't even imagine. Even the windows are the perfect skinniness, the brass rail holding the curtain the perfect height. They thought of everything. We had one drink, shared the croquettes (a special), the goat cheese salad (oozing with flavor and love), the steak frites (we wanted to lick the plate), and the chocolate cake (best in the city?). They didn't have ketchup for the fries ("We'll get some, just don't have it yet") but honestly? The fries were better without. Don't get ketchup, y'all! Entire bill was like $110-120 including tax but pre tip? Now this was the first Saturday night they were open ... ever ... with no hours posted and no reservations taken and no announcements made  ... and place was stacked, packed, throbbing, full of life, full of energy, full of a beautiful beaming love. I give three red 100 emojis. It's the perfect new dinner spot for fans of Union on Ossington or Cote de Boeuf on Queen (which the owner / chef of this crew also run as I understand).... a true gem that will not be very secret for very long. Hats off to Eamon, Teo, the tattooed bartender whose name I forgot, the amazing servers, the guys crushing it in that tiny kitchen, and everyone involved in this masterpiece !!!"
